---
title: "Simplified Double ML for Causal Inference"
date: "2026-06-12"
categories: [causal inference, machine learning]
---

## Background

Double machine learning (DML) is one of the most influential ideas to come out of econometrics in the last decade. It lets you estimate a causal effect while using flexible machine learning to soak up confounding — random forests, gradient boosting, the lasso, whatever — and still get a $\sqrt{n}$-consistent estimate with honest standard errors. That combination, ML flexibility *plus* valid inference, is what made it spread so quickly.

The catch is that the literature is written in a dialect. The foundational paper (Chernozhukov et al., 2018) and excellent reviews like Knaus (2022) speak the language of *programme evaluation*: programmes indexed by $w$, average potential outcomes, nuisance parameters, scores, Neyman orthogonality. None of it is wrong, but it can obscure what is, at heart, a simple recipe that any practitioner who has run a [propensity-score analysis](https://vyasenov.github.io/blog/flavors-prop-score-methods.html) will recognize. My goal in this note is to translate the core of DML — estimation of average treatment effects — into the plainer causal-inference vocabulary most readers already carry around. I keep the heterogeneous-effects and policy-learning machinery for another day; this is the basic, average-effects version.

If you read my recent post on [targeted learning](https://vyasenov.github.io/blog/tmle-estimation-recipe.html), much of this will feel familiar. DML and TMLE are siblings: both build on the same doubly robust score and both use sample splitting to license machine learning. They differ mainly in *how* they combine the pieces, not in the underlying logic.

## Notation

Consider the canonical setup: a binary treatment $D \in \{0, 1\}$, an outcome $Y$, and a vector of pre-treatment confounders $X$. Each unit has potential outcomes $Y(1)$ and $Y(0)$, and the target is the average treatment effect

$$\tau = \mathbb{E}\big[Y(1) - Y(0)\big].$$

Two functions of the data — the *nuisance parameters*, in the paper's language — carry the weight. The first is the **outcome regression**,

$$\mu_d(x) = \mathbb{E}[Y \mid D = d, X = x],$$

and the second is the **propensity score**,

$$e(x) = \mathbb{P}(D = 1 \mid X = x).$$

As usual, $\tau$ is causally identified under unconfoundedness ($Y(d) \perp D \mid X$), overlap ($0 < e(x) < 1$), and SUTVA. I take those as given and focus on estimation.

## A Closer Look

### A Short Dictionary

Before the method, here is the translation that unlocks most of the program-evaluation literature. None of these are new concepts — they are renamed versions of things you already use.

| Program-evaluation term | Causal-inference reading |
|---|---|
| Programme $w$ | Treatment arm / treatment level |
| Average potential outcome (APO), $\gamma_w$ | Mean potential outcome, $\mathbb{E}[Y(d)]$ |
| Nuisance parameters | The outcome regression $\mu_d$ and propensity score $e$ |
| Outcome / exposure mechanism | Outcome model / treatment-assignment model |
| Doubly robust score $\Gamma$ | The AIPW influence function (per-unit "pseudo-outcome") |
| Cross-fitting | Sample splitting to estimate nuisances out-of-fold |
| Neyman orthogonality | Insensitivity of the estimator to small nuisance errors |

With that dictionary in hand, the rest is short.

---

### The Doubly Robust Score

Everything in DML for average effects is built on one object: the **doubly robust score**, better known to many as the augmented inverse probability weighting (AIPW) score of Robins et al. (1994). For each unit it combines the outcome regression with an [inverse-probability-weighted](https://vyasenov.github.io/blog/flavors-prop-score-methods.html) correction:

$$
\psi_i = \underbrace{\mu_1(X_i) - \mu_0(X_i)}_{\text{outcome-regression guess}} + \underbrace{\frac{D_i\big(Y_i - \mu_1(X_i)\big)}{e(X_i)} - \frac{(1 - D_i)\big(Y_i - \mu_0(X_i)\big)}{1 - e(X_i)}}_{\text{IPW bias correction on the residuals}}.
$$

The ATE estimate is simply the sample average, $\hat\tau = \frac{1}{n}\sum_i \psi_i$, and — strikingly — its standard error is just the standard deviation of the $\psi_i$ divided by $\sqrt{n}$, with no adjustment for the fact that the nuisances were estimated. A one-sample $t$-test does the rest.

The name "doubly robust" comes from the structure: if the outcome regression $\mu_d$ is correct, the correction term has mean zero and the first part delivers the answer; if instead the propensity score $e$ is correct, the correction term repairs a wrong outcome model. You only need *one* of the two to be right. This is the same insurance policy that TMLE buys — DML just packages it differently.

---

### Cross-Fitting

There is one trap. If you estimate $\mu_d$ and $e$ with flexible machine learning on the *same* data you then plug into the score, the overfitting introduces a bias that does not vanish fast enough, and the $\sqrt{n}$ guarantee collapses. The fix is **cross-fitting**: estimate the nuisances on part of the data and evaluate the score on the held-out part, so no observation is used to predict its own nuisance values.

::: {.callout-note title="Algorithm: DML for the ATE"}
1. **Split** the sample into $K$ folds (5 is typical).
2. For each fold $k$: **fit** the outcome model $\hat\mu_d$ and propensity model $\hat e$ on the *other* $K-1$ folds, then **predict** $\hat\mu_1(X_i)$, $\hat\mu_0(X_i)$, $\hat e(X_i)$ for the units in fold $k$.
3. **Build** the doubly robust score $\psi_i$ for every unit using its out-of-fold predictions.
4. **Average**: $\hat\tau = \frac{1}{n}\sum_i \psi_i$, with $\widehat{\text{SE}} = \text{sd}(\psi_i)/\sqrt{n}$.
:::

The code below runs the whole recipe on simulated data, deliberately using random forests for both nuisances to show that the ML choice is a plug-in decision.

::::{.panel-tabset}

### R
```r
set.seed(1988)
library(ranger)   # install.packages("ranger")

# ---- Simulate confounded data ----
n <- 2000; p <- 5
X <- matrix(rnorm(n * p), n, p); colnames(X) <- paste0("X", 1:p)
e   <- plogis(0.8 * X[, 1] - 0.5 * X[, 2])          # true propensity
D   <- rbinom(n, 1, e)
mu0 <- X[, 1] + X[, 2] + X[, 3] + 0.5 * X[, 1] * X[, 2]
tau <- 2                                            # true ATE
Y   <- mu0 + tau * D + rnorm(n)

# ---- 5-fold cross-fitting of the two nuisances ----
folds <- sample(rep(1:5, length.out = n))
mu1_hat <- mu0_hat <- e_hat <- numeric(n)
XD <- cbind(D = D, X)                               # predictors for the outcome model
for (k in 1:5) {
  tr <- which(folds != k); te <- which(folds == k)
  # outcome model mu_d(x) = E[Y | D, X]
  om <- ranger(x = XD[tr, ], y = Y[tr])
  mu1_hat[te] <- predict(om, data = cbind(D = 1, X[te, ]))$predictions
  mu0_hat[te] <- predict(om, data = cbind(D = 0, X[te, ]))$predictions
  # propensity e(x) = P(D = 1 | X)
  ps <- ranger(x = X[tr, ], y = factor(D[tr]), probability = TRUE)
  e_hat[te] <- predict(ps, data = X[te, ])$predictions[, "1"]
}
e_hat <- pmin(pmax(e_hat, 0.01), 0.99)              # stabilize extreme weights

# ---- Doubly robust (AIPW) score, then average ----
psi <- (mu1_hat - mu0_hat) +
       D * (Y - mu1_hat) / e_hat -
       (1 - D) * (Y - mu0_hat) / (1 - e_hat)
cat(sprintf("DML ATE: %.3f (SE %.3f), true = %d\n",
            mean(psi), sd(psi) / sqrt(n), tau))
```

### Python
```python
import numpy as np
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestRegressor, RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.model_selection import KFold

np.random.seed(1988)

# ---- Simulate confounded data ----
n, p = 2000, 5
X   = np.random.normal(size=(n, p))
e   = 1 / (1 + np.exp(-(0.8 * X[:, 0] - 0.5 * X[:, 1])))   # true propensity
D   = np.random.binomial(1, e)
mu0 = X[:, 0] + X[:, 1] + X[:, 2] + 0.5 * X[:, 0] * X[:, 1]
tau = 2.0                                                  # true ATE
Y   = mu0 + tau * D + np.random.normal(size=n)

# ---- 5-fold cross-fitting of the two nuisances ----
mu1_hat, mu0_hat, e_hat = np.zeros(n), np.zeros(n), np.zeros(n)
XD = np.column_stack([X, D])                               # predictors for the outcome model
for tr, te in KFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=1988).split(X):
    # outcome model mu_d(x) = E[Y | D, X]
    om = RandomForestRegressor(n_estimators=200, min_samples_leaf=5,
                               random_state=1988).fit(XD[tr], Y[tr])
    mu1_hat[te] = om.predict(np.column_stack([X[te], np.ones(len(te))]))
    mu0_hat[te] = om.predict(np.column_stack([X[te], np.zeros(len(te))]))
    # propensity e(x) = P(D = 1 | X)
    ps = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=200, min_samples_leaf=5,
                                random_state=1988).fit(X[tr], D[tr])
    e_hat[te] = ps.predict_proba(X[te])[:, 1]

e_hat = np.clip(e_hat, 0.01, 0.99)                         # stabilize extreme weights

# ---- Doubly robust (AIPW) score, then average ----
psi = ((mu1_hat - mu0_hat)
       + D * (Y - mu1_hat) / e_hat
       - (1 - D) * (Y - mu0_hat) / (1 - e_hat))
print(f"DML ATE: {psi.mean():.3f} (SE {psi.std(ddof=1)/np.sqrt(n):.3f}), true = {tau}")
```
::::

---

### Why It Works: Neyman Orthogonality

The reason this whole construction holds together is a property called **Neyman orthogonality**. Loosely, the doubly robust score is built so that small errors in the estimated nuisances have only a *second-order* effect on the ATE estimate — the first-order sensitivity is zero. A naive outcome-regression plug-in does not have this property: a small bias in $\hat\mu_d$ passes straight through to the effect.

The practical consequence is what the literature calls *rate double robustness*. Because the score is insensitive to nuisance errors, you can estimate $\mu_d$ and $e$ with machine learning that converges *slower* than the usual parametric $\sqrt{n}$ rate — $n^{1/4}$ each is enough, since what matters is that the *product* of their error rates beats $n^{-1/2}$. Cross-fitting removes the overfitting bias; Neyman orthogonality removes the sensitivity to whatever bias remains. Together they let you bolt flexible ML onto a causal estimand and still hand back a $\sqrt{n}$-consistent, asymptotically normal, efficient estimate. Everything beyond average effects — the DR-learner for heterogeneous effects, optimal policy rules — is built on this very same score, which is exactly why the framework scales so gracefully.

## Bottom Line

- DML is, at its core, the old doubly robust (AIPW) estimator plus two modern ingredients: cross-fitting and a Neyman-orthogonal score.
- The recipe for the ATE is four lines: cross-fit an outcome model and a propensity model, assemble the per-unit doubly robust score, and average it; the standard error is just the score's sample standard deviation over $\sqrt{n}$.
- Double robustness means you need only *one* of the two nuisance models to be correct; rate double robustness means you can estimate both with slow-converging machine learning and still get valid $\sqrt{n}$ inference.
- The dense program-evaluation vocabulary hides a simple idea. Once you map "programme," "average potential outcome," and "nuisance parameter" onto treatment, mean potential outcome, and the two familiar regressions, the rest follows.

## Where to Learn More

The original and indispensable reference is Chernozhukov et al. (2018), which introduced the DML framework, Neyman-orthogonal scores, and cross-fitting. Knaus (2022), the basis for this note, is an unusually readable review that walks through DML-based program evaluation end to end, from average effects to heterogeneous effects and policy learning. For the doubly robust score itself, the lineage runs back to Robins et al. (1994). And for a closely related estimator that shares the same doubly robust foundation, see my post on [targeted learning](https://vyasenov.github.io/blog/tmle-estimation-recipe.html).

## References

- Chernozhukov, V., Chetverikov, D., Demirer, M., Duflo, E., Hansen, C., Newey, W., & Robins, J. (2018). Double/debiased machine learning for treatment and structural parameters. *The Econometrics Journal*, 21(1), C1–C68.

- Knaus, M. C. (2022). Double machine learning-based programme evaluation under unconfoundedness. *The Econometrics Journal*, 25(3), 602–627.

- Robins, J. M., Rotnitzky, A., & Zhao, L. P. (1994). Estimation of regression coefficients when some regressors are not always observed. *Journal of the American Statistical Association*, 89(427), 846–866.
